response: Pharmacies may return preps to stock if not picked up. Call the pharmacy to re-fill it, and call us if you need a replacement order.
If your usual prep is out of stock, we can switch you to a different prep (often Suprep) and send updated instructions.
If your prescription isn’t available by ~2 PM the day before, call us immediately—there may be a fallback option (like Dulcolax + Miralax) or we may switch your prep.
Clear broth (chicken/beef/vegetable) and bone broth are typically okay the day before, as long as there are no solids/particles.
Clear juice without pulp can be okay. If the juice has pulp or particles, avoid it.
The day before a colonoscopy is clear liquids only (no solid food). Follow the office instructions, and nothing by mouth after midnight.
For high-volume/gallon prep (option 4), an anti-nausea med (often Reglan) may be prescribed. Take it ~30 minutes before starting prep.
Metoclopramide (Reglan) is an anti-nausea medication sometimes prescribed with high-volume prep to prevent nausea.
If you need an anti-nausea refill for the prep, call us with your pharmacy name/location and we can review and resend if appropriate.
If you are significantly constipated, Dr. Shelby may choose a higher-volume PEG jug prep to ensure adequate cleanout.
Correct—no solid foods the day before. Clear liquids only (broth, approved-color Jello, clear juice without pulp).
Avoid dairy on clear liquid day. Black coffee/tea with sugar is usually okay; no milk/cream.
For endoscopy: you may have dinner, but no solid food after 8 PM, and nothing to drink after midnight.
Correct—for endoscopy with anesthesia: last solid meal before 8 PM and nothing to drink after midnight.
Broth is okay as long as it is clear with no particles/solids. Strain it if needed.
We can resend instructions by email and also by text in a simpler format. Tell us which is easiest for you.
Typically your last sip of liquids is by midnight, then nothing by mouth after midnight due to anesthesia.
The split-dose powder/liquid prep is usually two smaller doses totaling about 64 oz (plus extra clear liquids). Follow the office schedule for timing.
A low-residue diet for a few days before can help (avoid salads, heavy vegetables, and high-fiber foods). Follow your office instructions for timing.
Many options include Miralax around 6 PM and 7 PM to ensure full cleanout. Use your office instruction sheet for exact timing.
Option 5 is usually the fallback if your prescription prep isn’t available in time. If you have your prescription prep, follow the assigned option.
If you’re picking up samples/forms, that’s usually at the office front desk in Suite 110 (not the surgery center in Suite 100).
Correct—many split-dose preps are taken at 2 PM and 8 PM the day before, with additional clear liquids and sometimes Miralax as instructed.
Many patients find Plenvu easier than other options, though cost/coverage can vary. We can help choose the best prep for you.
Yes—on prep day, once you wake up, it’s clear liquids only for the entire day until you start prep dosing later.
If you’re severely constipated, Dr. Shelby may choose a higher-volume prep to ensure you’re fully cleaned out. Call us if you’re concerned before starting.
Please follow the option your office instructions specify. If you’re unsure which option you were assigned, call us and we’ll confirm based on your chart.
If prep was started and the procedure was rescheduled, call us for updated timing and whether you need replacement meds (including anti-nausea).
In some cases we can provide a prep sample box, especially if coverage is limited. Call us to confirm availability.
Correct—typically nothing by mouth after midnight due to anesthesia. Drink adequate clear liquids earlier in the day before midnight.
We can resend your prep instructions. Please check spam/junk as well, and tell us whether you prefer email or text.
We can resend instructions by email and text. If you can’t read the email, let us know and we’ll text the key steps.
We generally advise avoiding seeds/very fibrous foods a few days before colonoscopy. If you have specific instructions, follow them; otherwise call us to confirm.
Many patients find the powder/liquid split-dose prep easier than swallowing 24 tablets. We can offer options based on your preference and history.
Tablet preps can cause nausea for some patients. Taking 1–2 tablets at a time with plenty of water and using anti-nausea meds can help; call us if severe.
If your instructions say split dosing, do not take both doses the same day unless we specifically instruct you. Call us to confirm your exact plan.
For endoscopy only: you may have dinner the night before, but no solid food after 8 PM, and nothing to drink after midnight (unless instructed otherwise).
Ensure Clear is typically okay if it is labeled “clear.” Avoid drinks with red/purple/blue dye.
Jello is okay if it’s clear, yellow, or orange. Avoid red, purple, and blue dyes.
Many options require OTC Miralax between doses (commonly around 6 PM and 7 PM). Please use the schedule from the office instructions.
Miralax powder is preferred for reliability. If you bought chews, please call us—we may advise switching to powder.
Typically nothing by mouth after midnight (no water) because of anesthesia. Follow the instructions you received for your specific case.
For endoscopy with anesthesia: no solid food after 8 PM and nothing to drink after midnight. If you were told no sedation, confirm instructions with us.
Colonoscopy can still be done during your period. Use a tampon if you’re comfortable.
If samples are available, you can usually pick them up at our office front desk in Suite 110 during business hours.
Please follow the office prep instructions (not the box directions). If anything is unclear, call us and we’ll walk you through it.
We offer pills (24 tablets total) or a split-dose drink/powder. Many patients find the drink easier than swallowing 24 tablets, but it’s personal preference.
Plenvu is taken in two doses (often around 2 PM and 8 PM). The box can have multiple pouches—use the office instructions for the correct pouch sequence.
SuTab is typically 24 tablets total, split into two doses (often 12 at 2 PM and 12 at 8 PM) with additional liquids/Miralax as instructed.
Clear liquids include water, clear broth, approved-color Jello, and clear juices without pulp. Avoid red/purple/blue dyes and any liquids with solids/particles.
If you need to pick something up (forms/samples), please come during business hours. Call us and we’ll confirm today’s cutoff time.
If Medicare doesn’t cover a prep, the office may have samples available in some cases. You’ll still need OTC Miralax if instructed.
Most colonoscopy preps start at 2 PM the day before the procedure (then additional steps later in the evening). Follow the office instructions you were sent.
If prescribed, take the anti-nausea pill (Reglan or Zofran) about 30 minutes before starting the prep.
If stools are watery with no solid pieces, you should still come in as scheduled. If you’re seeing solid stool or you’re unsure, call us right away.
Both Reglan and Zofran can be used for nausea depending on the prep. If you’re unsure which you should use, call us and we’ll confirm your plan.

Procedures are performed under anesthesia and usually last about one hour. Patients must complete a bowel prep the day before, have nothing to eat or drink after midnight, and bring a responsible adult driver.
